clinical_history: 51 year-old man with complaint of progressive shortness of breath for over a year. This ECG is most consistent with which ONE of the following diagnoses?
diagnosis: This ECG shows atrial fibrillation with a relatively rapid ventricular response rate of about 115 bpm.  There is an indeterminate to rightward QRS axis and a right ventricular conduction delay (rSr~S~~ pattern in V1 and V2). This combination suggests a right ventricular overload syndrome. Prominent lateral precordial voltage raises consideration of biventricular hypertrophy. Non-specific ST-T wave changes are also present. The constellation of atrial fibrillation and right or biventricular hypertrophy, while not specific, is a classic combination in rheumatic heart disease. A transthoracic echo showed marked left atrial enlargement and stasis, along with findings that were typical of rheumatic mitral valve deformity and stenosis, and a reduced left ventricular ejection fraction of 40-46~.   The patient underwent cardiac catheterization and was found to have severe mitral stenosis with a mean gradient of 15mmHg and calculated value area of 0.68 cm squared and underwent successful balloon-catheter mitral valvuloplasty via a trans-septal approach.
